status: Run 2026-06-26. Fluree v4.1.0 vs Amazon Neptune 1.4.7.0 on matched AWS Graviton4 (r8g) instances at 4 sizes (32/64/128/256 GB). SPARQLoscope 105-query suite + dataset load. Fluree passes 105/105 at every size (16.6-26.7 ms geomean) and loads 9-15x faster; Neptune is non-functional below 128 GB (10-16/105), crosses over at 128 GB (81/105), and plateaus at 256 GB (84/105). RAM buys completion (fewer OOMs), not query speed. Neptune required skolemization of ~42% blank-node triples to load at all.

dataset.name: DBLP-core (bibliography only)
dataset.neptune_preprocessing: skolemized _:label -> IRI (Neptune bulk loader rejects blank nodes; ~42% of triples affected). Fluree loaded raw N-Triples natively.

methodology.silicon: AWS Graviton4 (r8g, 8 GB/vCPU) at all sizes
methodology.fluree: v4.1.0 official installer (native, aarch64), on EC2 r8g.*
methodology.neptune: 1.4.7.0 on db.r8g.*, queried from in-VPC client (cheap t4g.medium for 256 GB)
methodology.notes: Neptune default query timeout (120s) raised to 180s via custom cluster parameter group for parity. 256 GB Neptune cluster was resized in-place from 128 GB (data preserved, no reload). 32 GB Neptune pass-count is cache-state-sensitive (noisy 9-48); reported as 16/105 with caveat.

neptune.load_note: resized in-place from 128 GB cluster; no reload

caveats: Not bit-comparable to the same-box dblp-core run (different hardware family, engine versions, skolemized Neptune input).
Neptune query latency is RAM-independent: completed queries run the same time at 32 GB and 256 GB; RAM only converts OOM/timeout to completion.
~21 queries (exists-join-3-*, exists-join-2-large-large-with-large-result, etc.) time out on Neptune at every size.
64 GB Neptune passes fewer than 32 GB (10 vs 16), reproduced across two runs (per-thread memory -> more OOMs on the 2xlarge).
Neptune required skolemization to load DBLP-core's blank nodes; Fluree did not.
